issue_title: numpy arange unsupported
issue_body:
Versions:

* `python`: v3.6.0
* `numpy`: v1.12.0
* `pint`: 0.8.dev0 (commit de8fdff)

```python
import numpy as np
import pint
ureg = pint.UnitRegistry()
x = 1 * ureg.meter

y = np.arange(0, x, 0.1)
```

Results in:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "pint_test.py", line 7, in <module>
    y = np.arange(0, x, 0.1)
  File "/home/will/src/pint/pint/quantity.py", line 431, in __float__
    raise DimensionalityError(self._units, 'dimensionless')
pint.errors.DimensionalityError: Cannot convert from 'meter' to 'dimensionless'
```

Since one can do `np.linspace(0, x, 10)` without problems I think this is a bug, although I may be misunderstanding and this may be an implication of the stated [numpy support](https://pint.readthedocs.io/en/0.6/numpy.html#support)

issue_title: ReadTheDocs spacing is off for providers
issue_body:
http://fake-factory.readthedocs.org/en/latest/providers/internet.html

The spacing makes this pretty unreadable without constantly scrolling back and forth, which is annoying. 

Since these are the official extended docs linked to in the project ReadMe, it'd certainly be aesthetically nicer if they used native RTD formatting, for example see the formatting for the reference page of the factory boy project: http://factoryboy.readthedocs.org/en/latest/reference.html

I dug around a little trying to make a pull request to fix this, but it looks like Faker's docs are auto-generated somehow and I didn't have time to figure it all out. If you point me in the right direction, I'd be happy to submit a PR.

issue_title: No PPI Support
issue_body:
_From [pedrogfr...@gmail.com](https://code.google.com/u/105103961488205321035/) on August 17, 2010 13:00:57_

What steps will reproduce the problem? 1. Get a PCAP with PPI info (e.g., in Kismet)
2. Try to open it using dpkt with the code on http://www.jjguy.com/pcaputils/ What is the expected output? What do you see instead? I see:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "parser2.py", line 4, in \<module>
    pcapReader = dpkt.pcap.Reader(file(sys.argv[1], "rb"))
  File "/usr/lib/pymodules/python2.6/dpkt/pcap.py", line 105, in __init__
    self.dloff = dltoff[self.__fh.linktype]
KeyError: 192 What version of the product are you using? On what operating system? 1.6+svn54-1 on Ubuntu 10.04 Please provide any additional information below. http://nmap.org/svn/libpcap/pcap-common.c defines 'LINKTYPE_PPI' as '192'. http://www.cacetech.com/documents/PPI%20Header%20format%201.0.9.pdf provides PPI spec.

NOTE: even if it isn't worth it to support PPI may I sugest a better error message for it than an exception?

_Original issue: http://code.google.com/p/dpkt/issues/detail?id=45_

issue_title: vrrp.py has an initialization bug for the offset variable
issue_body:
_From [timur.al...@gmail.com](https://code.google.com/u/117790548747779378711/) on March 18, 2012 23:45:23_

What steps will reproduce the problem? 1. Input packet:
p = '\x01\x00\x5e\x00\x00\x12\x00\x00\x5e\x00\x01\x36\x81\x00\x0a\x6c\x08\x00\x45\x00\x00\x1c\xff\xa2\x00\x00\xff\x70\x2a\x29\xfd\xf4\xe6\x9c\xd2\xda\xda\x39\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00'

2. Parse with dpkt.ethernet.Ethernet(p)

3. There is a parsing error:
File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/dpkt/vrrp.py", line 37, in unpack
    self.auth = self.data[off+4:]

The fix is to initialize off to 0 before the for loop. I attached the patch that fixes that. Anyone sees any problems with it?

**Attachment:** [vrrp.patch](http://code.google.com/p/dpkt/issues/detail?id=87)

_Original issue: http://code.google.com/p/dpkt/issues/detail?id=87_

issue_title: Parallel implementation
issue_body:
Hello again,

The K-Prototypes algorithm continues to intrigue me, so I played around with it some more. I did the Cython implementation before, as discussed in #57, but the changes required for that were so far-reaching I can imagine merging the back into this repository is challenging.

In the meantime, I implemented both K-Modes and K-Prototypes in a parallel fashion, basically copying the strategy applied in scikit-learn, namely doing multiple runs with different initializations in parallel using joblib.
Minimal code changes are required to accomplish this, so it might make a better initial candidate to improve performance. Most changes are related to the way the algorithms deal with random variables, as this becomes a little more difficult to keep reproducible when adding threading into the mix.

I did some testing on a dataset that I unfortunately cannot share, the results of which are in this gist:
https://gist.github.com/rphes/48569eb0c929d33deef18c9de0d96aa8

Interesting observations are that K-Prototypes benefits from multithreading whereas K-Modes really does not. I think this might be due to the very low complexity of the K-Modes algorithm, making it memory-bound. Threading therefore only introduces additional overhead due to the forking process and resource contention between threads.
K-Prototypes performs some actual computations, so it is less affected by this, but still we see performance increase level off at 4 cores, presumable for the same reason.

Now, if you'd like to incorporate this stuff, I'm more than happy to submit a PR, with a parallel version of K-Prototypes, or both algorithms with added tests and documentation. The only caveat is that users need to consider whether their use-case is actually going to see performance increase when throwing more cores at it, as my example shows.
The API is completely backwards compatible, so that should not be an issue.

Check out the code here:
https://github.com/rphes/kmodes/tree/parallel
and let me know what you think!

issue_title: Cannot distinguish in-doubt messages when the link is closed.
issue_body: When a sender link closes, all queued messages have their callback invoked with the state == ABORTED.  The correct behavior would only pass the aborted state for those messages that are in-doubt (sent but never acked).  Messages that have been queued but never sent (due to credit) should not be aborted.  Rather the callback should set the RELEASED state, which allows the application to re-send the messages safely.

issue_title: pgmagick doesn't release GIL
issue_body:
The idea is that image processing library could release global interpreter lock on heavy computing when it isn't interacting with python's interpretation structures. Most of the python libraries do this, while pgmagick doesn't.

This is the test case:

```python
from __future__ import print_function

import sys
import time
import threading
from io import BytesIO

try:
    from PIL import Image
except ImportError:
    Image = None
try:
    import cv2
except ImportError:
    cv2 = None
try:
    from wand.image import Image as WandImage
except ImportError:
    WandImage = None
try:
    from pgmagick import Image as PGImage, FilterTypes, Blob, Geometry
except ImportError:
    PGImage = None
try:
    from pyvips import Image as VipsImage
except ImportError:
    VipsImage = None


imagename = sys.argv[1]


def deferred_noop():
    time.sleep(.2)


def deferred_pillow():
    im = Image.open(imagename)
    im.resize((1024, 768), Image.BICUBIC)
    im.save(BytesIO(), format='jpeg', quality=85)


def deferred_opencv():
    im = cv2.imread(imagename)
    cv2.resize(im, (1024, 768), interpolation=cv2.INTER_AREA)
    cv2.imencode(".jpeg", im, [int(cv2.IMWRITE_JPEG_QUALITY), 85])


def deferred_wand():
    with WandImage(filename=imagename) as im:
        im.resize(1024, 768, 'catrom')
        im.compression_quality = 85
        im.format = 'jpeg'
        im.save(file=BytesIO())


def deferred_pgmagick():
    im = PGImage(imagename)

    im.filterType(FilterTypes.CatromFilter)
    im.zoom(Geometry(1024, 768))

    im.quality(85)
    im.magick('jpeg')
    im.write(Blob())


def deferred_vips():
    im = VipsImage.new_from_file(imagename)
    im.resize(0.4, kernel='cubic')
    im.write_to_buffer('.jpeg', Q=85)



def test_deferred(deferred):
    start = time.time()
    t = threading.Thread(target=deferred)
    t.start()
    n = 0

    while t.isAlive():
        time.sleep(.001)
        n += 1

    print('>>> {:<20} time: {:1.3f}s  {:>3} switches'.format(
        deferred.__name__, time.time() - start, n))


test_deferred(deferred_noop)
if not Image is None:
    test_deferred(deferred_pillow)
if not cv2 is None:
    test_deferred(deferred_opencv)
if not WandImage is None:
    test_deferred(deferred_wand)
if not PGImage is None:
    test_deferred(deferred_pgmagick)
if not VipsImage is None:
    test_deferred(deferred_vips)
```

And my results:

```
>>> deferred_noop        time: 0.202s  145 switches
>>> deferred_pillow      time: 0.140s   63 switches
>>> deferred_opencv      time: 0.181s  154 switches
>>> deferred_wand        time: 0.225s  194 switches
>>> deferred_pgmagick    time: 0.096s    0 switches
>>> deferred_vips        time: 0.088s   78 switches
```

issue_title: psutil.Popen returns the wrong exit code when it is killed with a signal
issue_body:
# Summary

On Linux when a process gets killed by a signal the exit code is negative and the abs value of the exit code is the signal number (e.g. `-6` is `SIGABRT`).

Python's `subprocess.Popen` will return the correct negative exit code if the application is killed with a signal. `pustil.Popen` does not.

I think this is bug because `psutil.Popen` is supposed to have the same interface as `subprocess.Popen`.

Either this is a bug which should be fixed or it is intentional behaviour. If it intentional is should really be documented.

I found this stackoverflow posted about this problem from a few years back but I'm not sure if the OP ever reported this problem.

http://stackoverflow.com/questions/25639578/psutil-subprocess-return-code-when-process-killed

# How to reproduce

I did this use `psutil==5.0.1` installed with `pip`.

Write a python program that aborts.

`abort.py`
```python
import os
os.abort()
```

Now in an interactive python console try running it with `subprocess.Popen`

```
>>> import subprocess
>>> x = subprocess.Popen(['python', 'abort.py'])
>>> x.wait()
-6
```

Try running with `psutil.Popen`
```
>>> import psutil
>>> x = psutil.Popen(['python', 'abort.py'])
>>> x.wait()
6
```

You can see that the exit code from `subprocess.Popen` is negative indicating it was killed by a signal. `psutil.Popen` returns a positive exit code so it looks like the process was not killed by a signal even though it was!

issue_title: Dotdrop does not work with macOS provided readlink
issue_body: GNU's readlink has the `-f` flag, but this option is not supported by every implementation, like the macOS implementation of readlink. Currently dotdrop uses `readlink -f` to get the directory where `dotdrop.sh` lives, meaning that the script does not start on macOS. It would be nice if dotdrop would not depend on this behaviour, so it also works on systems that do not have GNU readlink, but the BSD variants.

issue_title: Decorator module dependency problem
issue_body:
The _decorator_ module prior to version **4.0.0** does not implement the `decorate` function. Distributions like Ubuntu Trusty or Debian Jessie come with decorator **3.4.0** which satisfies the requirements, but leads to a broken package.

To avoid the problem, you could either specify minimum _decorator_ version as 4.0.0 (so that it's upgraded from pypi when needed), or use the old way of creating decorated functions, that is returning: `decorator(wrapper, function)` instead of: `decorate(function, wrapper)`. According to the [documentation](http://decorator.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tests.documentation.html#what-s-new-in-version-4) it does the same job, but is deprecated and available only "for now" (almost 3 years now :) ).

issue_title: "Mixed" auth methods - headers and cookies

Since I would like to use the same endpoint for browsers (that can use cookies) and other consumers (that doesn't use cookies, like a simple cURL call), is there a way to accomplish this "mixed" behavior? I can help with PRs, if needed.

issue_title: Leading and Trailing Spaces on Package XML Strings
issue_body: When reading strings from `package.xml`, such as package dependencies, the returned strings should be trimmed. Leading and trailing spaces create unknown package names, which leads to a crash when sorting the packages in topological order.

issue_title: Convert mnemonics to upper/lower case
issue_body:
Provide an option to ignore case for mnemonics, so that duplicates work properly. e.g.

```
~C
Depth.m   : metres
GR.cps    : gamma
CALI.mm   : diameter
CALI.mm   : diameter 2nd run
gr.API    : gamma calibrated
```

produces mnemonics `Depth, GR, CALI:1, CALI:2, gr` at the moment. With the option enabled, it would produce `DEPTH, GR:1, CALI:1, CALI:2, GR:2`.

issue_title: MusicControls plugin doesn't notice app being killed
issue_body:
If Mopidy Mobile is running in the background and gets "killed" after some period of inactivity, the MuciControls notification will no longer get updated, and prev/play/pause/next buttons will no longer work.
Clicking the track image/MoMo icon *will* restart the app, though, and after selecting a server the notification plugin seems to work correctly, again.
See also https://github.com/homerours/cordova-music-controls-plugin/issues/53

issue_title: Mistletoe logo
issue_body:
Hi,

first of all, thanks for the tool: it's a piece of cake for markdown parsing (and also for custom rendering)!

Just for the sake of precision, I'd like to point out a common misconception about mistletoe, which is not the plant depicted in the logo. The logo plant is either the [Ruscus aculeatus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/), also called *butcher's-broom* or *christmas berry* or the [Ilex aquifolium](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ilex_aquifolium), called *christmas holly*. Instead, the **mistletoe** is the [Viscum album](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mistletoe), which has white berries and has plain thick leaves (not spiny as the other two plants).

I'd like also to make clear that letting you change the logo is not my purpose, but I'd like only to friendly make you aware of which is the *“right“* **mistletoe**. :-)

All the best,

Luca

issue_title: Feature Request: cardinality
issue_body:
Hello everyone,
For one of my projects I would need to get the `cardinality` of a bit array, i.e the number of bits set to 1, [as defined in Java's BitSet class](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/7/docs/api/java/util/BitSet.html#cardinality())

For now the simplest way of doing this is using `sum`:
```python
cardinality = sum(1 for i in my_bitarray if i)
```

My issue is that it is no that fast (between 10 and 50ms for a million bits array on my machine), I mean not that fast if I need to compute/use it quite often.

Is there a better way to get the cardinality ?
I suggest the cardinality to be an attribute and incrementing/decrementing it when setting a bit to 1

issue_title: Validation of Strings
Handle Unicode strings with true format string logic
issue_body:
Regex, when doing format replacements, allows for `!s`, `!r`, and `!a` (though `!a` is omitted in PY2).

For completeness, add support for these even if it is unlikely that these would ever be used.  There isn't really a practical case for this, it would just be nice if these don't break things.

Regex actually applies these to the capture group except when specifying a sub capture `{1[2]}`.  This is weird and I don't care to mimic that, so this will apply to the captured string always.

Due to the complexity of handling string casing, we mimic formatting without actually calling the "format" function on the template's string object.  For this reason, formatting actually works in byte strings because our algorithm has no restriction in this case. Even though `{}` style formatting is not officially supported byte strings.

We will most likely implement things like they are in with Python 3.5's `%` style byte string formatting:

- `!r` == `repr(bstring).encode('ascii','backslashreplace')`
- `!a` will work like `!r`
- `!s` probably shouldn't actually be supported as `!b` would actually make more sense, but for now, we'll make this work like this: `bstring.encode('ascii','backslashreplace')` and keep things consistent and not add something new like `!b`.

For Unicode strings, we'll just do the expected things:

- `!s` == `str(string)`
- `!a` == `ascii(string)`
- `!r` == `repr(string)`

issue_title: zxcvbn is not thread safe
issue_body:
`zxcvbn.zxcvbn(password, user_inputs)` is not thread safe, because it calls `matching.set_user_input_dictionary(...)` which puts state into a module global variable `RANKED_DICTIONARIES`

This can lead to incorrect behaviour if two threads are concurrently calling this function with different user_inputs.

issue_title: Logic16 CommandNAKedError on save_to_file while in loop
issue_body:
I am attempting to write a test utility which will capture and save a new waveform every 20 seconds. After the first 2-3 successful iterations in the loop, the SaveDataToFile routine is raising CommandNAKedError which to my understanding of the docs means that the capture isn't complete when called or there is an issue with the filepath. As the first few files saved successfully, I doubt that the filepath is the issue, though watching the GUI the capture is definitely finished when I call save_to_file

I have attempted to wrap your library in a class of my own to allow for easy reuse. 

I was having trouble with the capture_start_and_wait_until_finished function, so I took the code in it and built my own which catches the NAK exception and ignores it. Is this the correct method for dealing with the NAK when calling this function before capture is complete? 

I'm using Python 3.5 / Logic 1.2.5

Here is the loop I am currently attempting to run and my class

```
l.StartCapture()
l.WaitUntilCaptureComplete()
l.SaveDataToFile(filepath + "\Error_" + str(count) + ".logicdata")
time.sleep(20)

```

```
class LogicAnalyzer:

    s = 0

    # Initializes the Saleae API
    def __init__(self, host='localhost', port=10429):
        self.s = saleae.Saleae(host,port)

        print("Connected to Logic")

        return

    # Starts the capture sequence
    def StartCapture(self):
        print("Starting capture")
        self.s.capture_start()
        return

    def WaitUntilCaptureComplete(self):
        done = False
        while done == False:
            try:	
                while not self.s.is_processing_complete():
                    print("\t..waiting for capture to complete")
                    time.sleep(1)

                print("Capture complete")

                done = True

            except self.s.CommandNAKedError:
                # This exception is raised if processing of capture is not complete on the
                # side of the analyzer. This is expected for the first few calls, therefore we 
                # simply ignore it until is_processing_complete returns true
                pass

    # Will save last capture in given output location/file
    # Filename must be absolute path
    # Folder must exist for file to be written
    def SaveDataToFile(self, filename):

        self.s.save_to_file(filename)
        print("File Saved")
        return

```

Not sure if it will help but I also made a log as requested in analog-io's thread. 
[logfile.txt](https://github.com/ppannuto/python-saleae/files/32217/logfile.txt)

issue_title: pexpect serial misses output
issue_body:
I have found a problem, where pexpect serial will miss some of the output in a command.

I am running a command over serial and expecting the prompt, then using the child.before variable to get what the command returned. The command returns a multi-line output, but it misses some of the output:

```python
import serial                                                                                                                                                                                                 
import sys                                                                                                                                                                                                    
from pexpect_serial import SerialSpawn                                                                                                                                                                        
ser = serial.Serial('/dev/ttyUSB1', 115200, timeout=0)                                                                                                                                                        
ss = SerialSpawn(ser, maxread=4096)                                                                                                                                                                           
ss.delaybeforesend = None                                                                                                                                                                                     
#ss.logfile = sys.stdout                                                                                                                                                                                      
ss.sendline('x version')                                                                                                                                                                                      
ss.expect('\w:\\\>')                                                                                                                                                                                          
print("'{}'".format(ss.before))                                                                                                                                                                               
ser.close() 
```

Correct output:
```
'x version
{
    "response": 
    {
        "status_code": "000",
        "status_text": "",
        "data" : 
        {
            "hw_version":"1",
            "machine":"root@2c0f20e6e095",
            "date":"Tue Sep 27 02:10:02 UTC 2016"
        }
    }
}
'
```

Incorrect output:
```
'x version
{
    "response": 
    {
        "status_code"dirty"
        }
    }
}
'
```

I don't know what I am doing wrong. It returns the correct output sometimes, but the rest of the time it misses some of the middle.

Note, newline characters are being sent as '\r\n' by the remote device. The expect line is expecting something like 'a:\>' (e.g. DOS)

If I `pip install pyOpenSSL==17.5.0` and then run the `pytest tests/main/test_https.py`, I get an exception that looks like this:
```
if isinstance(e.reason, _SSLError):
                # This branch is for urllib3 v1.22 and later.
>               raise SSLError(e, request=request)
E               requests.exceptions.SSLError: HTTPSConnectionPool(host='testme.org', port=443): Max retries exceeded with url: /json (Caused by SSLError(SSLError("bad handshake: SysCallError(9, 'EBADF')",),))
```

urllib3 does some things differently when it detects that the OpenSSL module is available. I found prior art in getting mocking of pyOpenSSL in HTTPretty (https://github.com/gabrielfalcao/HTTPretty/pull/215/files) but it looks pretty gnarly.

Is adding support for environments with PyOpenSSL installed anywhere on the roadmap?

issue_title: Duplicate key name in dictionary. How to solve?
empty array or plot axis dimension mismatch in silenceRemovel()
issue_body:
Just remind in my test case, the following two lines will result in T1 or T2 nan if L1 is 0 or 1:
T1 = numpy.mean(E[0:L1]) + 0.000000000000001                 
T2 = numpy.mean(E[-L1:-1]) + 0.000000000000001 
we can solve this according to the value of L1.

The plot will fail due to mismatch dimension in the following line of code:
plt.plot(numpy.arange(0, ProbOnset.shape[0] * stStep, stStep), ProbOnset)
I solved this by the following:
plt.plot(numpy.arange(0, ProbOnset.shape[0] * stStep - stStep/2, stStep), ProbOnset)

Thank you all the contributors for this wonderful tool.
Regards

issue_body:
As a user of the tiingo library, I would like to validate requested sampling frequencies so that users get immediate feedback when they used invalid frequencies. (This would have prevented #52).

To close this PR, I would like to see

- Updates to`get_ticker_price`. Perhaps use the `Enum` class in Python to help with this. Since this library supports Python2 and Python3, we may need the https://pypi.python.org/pypi/enum34 to ensure backwards compatibility
- Tests to cover the new lines of code
- Mention the supported frequencies in `README.md`

issue_title: "No API.AI Client Access Token set. You will be able to query the agent"
Understanding how to use CSRF tokens via cookies for `jwt_refresh_token_required` route
issue_body:
Hi @vimalloc ,

First, thanks for this great library !

I just have one question, I can't figure out how to properly use a `jwt_refresh_token_required` decorated route when setting CSRF tokens in cookies.

To be more specific, I set:
- `JWT_COOKIE_CSRF_PROTECT = True`
- `JWT_CSRF_IN_COOKIES = True`
- `JWT_ACCESS_CSRF_COOKIE_NAME = 'csrf_access_token'`
- `JWT_REFRESH_CSRF_COOKIE_NAME = 'csrf_refresh_token'`

When I use my `/login` route and then some `jwt_required` decorated data, everything works fine.
But when my acess token expires, I post on my `jwt_refresh_token_required` decorated route but there I get `"Missing CSRF token in headers"` 401 error.

Looking at the code, I can't figure out how to use CSRF token from cookie in this refresh route since the only way to get the token is from headers : https://github.com/vimalloc/flask-jwt-extended/blob/master/flask_jwt_extended/view_decorators.py#L176

Is there a way for this decorator to use directly CSRF token from cookies or do I need to explicitly pass the CSRF token in the header when making my http post ?

(I'm using version 3.4.0)

issue_title: shutdown delay with 0.2.5
issue_body:
I think there may be a regression in 0.2.5, which seems to wait for 5s on all shutdown for me.

[This code](https://github.com/jruere/multiprocessing-logging/blob/7fd2dcd74fe4a1f0f024ba4daa8be464bee66297/multiprocessing_logging.py#L66) should probably not execute on the receiving side based on [the documentation](https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/multiprocessing.html#multiprocessing.Queue.join_thread); indeed the docs suggest it's not needed on the sending side either, since it's the default behaviour.

For reference, here's how logbook does it:
https://github.com/getlogbook/logbook/blob/9d81786f505a5edabc14cfb2b7af6567c219e38e/logbook/queues.py#L493
...but there's no decent example to show how to wire those two together!

My guess is that there's some thread management issue lurking in multiprocessing-logging or there was a process being managed badly. Thoughts?

issue_title: Subclass TokenAuthentication to work
issue_body:
I had to subclass the TokenAuthentication class to make my view able to work on "Convert token" method (Making only few requests to facebook and using conver-token to work on it), change the field reference "key" to "token" and set the class property "model" to get_access_token_model(), from oauth2_provider.models file. If it's ok, I suggest you to add in the docs! Thanks!

**Python:** 2.7.x
**DRF:**  3.6.x
**Django:** 1.10.x
**Django-oauth-toolkit**: 1.0

```python

from oauth2_provider.models import get_access_token_model
from rest_framework.authentication import TokenAuthentication

...

class BearerTokenAuthentication(TokenAuthentication):
    keyword = u"Bearer"

    def authenticate_credentials(self, key):
        model = get_access_token_model()

        try:
            # here changing model.objects.select_related('user').get(key=key) to 
            # model.objects.select_related('user').get(token=key)
            token = model.objects.select_related('user').get(token=key)
        except model.DoesNotExist:
            raise exceptions.AuthenticationFailed(_('Invalid token.'))

        if not token.user.is_active:
            raise exceptions.AuthenticationFailed(_('User inactive or deleted.'))

        return (token.user, token)
```

issue_title: HTTP status should be 401 for expired token
issue_body:
The Read Me document states:

    Expiring Tokens works exactly the same as the default TokenAuth, except that using an expired token will return a response with an HTTP 400 status and a Token has expired error message.

HTTP response status 400 means “Bad request”, as in a *malformed* request. This does not seem appropriate for a well-formed request with expired authentication.

Instead, the response status 401 “Unauthorized” is more appropriate. The message “Token has expired” can still be in the response body to explain why the authentication failed.

(Reference for [HTTP response codes, temporary errors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_HTTP_status_codes#4xx_Client_errors))

issue_title: Support full configuration through command line parameters
issue_body:
When deploying my apps to production I usually build containers, and I was expecting to be able to separate the interface servers containers from the workers containers, since they have only the channel layer configuration in common. However, it seems Daphne relies exclusively on the channel layer configuration as can be found on the Django settings.

It seems to me that it should be possible to also pass the needed layer configuration through command line parameters to daphne, and this way have a completely independent package with no need to include application code.

issue_title: Inference with openblas and Ubuntu 14.04 hangs on C5 instances.
issue_body:
## Description
The default install of MXNet currently hangs when running most types of inference with a particular setup on C5 instances.  Any setup that has the openblas library that is, for example, installed with Ubuntu 14.04 will have this issue.  This issue may be effecting all hardware with Skylake architecture, but it is deterministically failing on C5s.

## Environment info (Required)
Ubuntu 14.04

## Steps to Reproduce:
Launch a C5 instance type with Docker support.
Build the following Dockerfile:
```Dockerfile
# -*- mode: dockerfile -*-
FROM ubuntu:14.04
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y build-essential git libopenblas-dev liblapack-dev \
    libopencv-dev libcurl4-openssl-dev libgtest-dev cmake wget unzip
RUN cd /usr/src/gtest && cmake CMakeLists.txt && make && cp *.a /usr/lib
RUN git clone --recursive https://github.com/dmlc/mxnet
RUN ln -s /usr/lib/libopenblas.so /usr/lib/libcblas.so
RUN cd mxnet && make USE_OPENCV=0 USE_CUDA=0 USE_CUDNN=0 -j$(nproc)
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y libmouse-perl pdl cpanminus swig libgraphviz-perl
RUN cpanm -q Function::Parameters Hash::Ordered
RUN cd mxnet && ./perl-package/test.sh
```

Package used (Python/R/Scala/Julia):
Perl for the test.  Also verified it fails with the same stack in Python.

MXNet commit hash:
fd45517614842bfa1d32d1ba54a200eb4a0dd377

## Error Message:
Only one frame with symbols on the stack:
```
#0  0x00002b260530cc93 in sgemm_kernel_PRESCOTT () from /usr/lib/libopenblas.so.0
```

## What have you tried to solve it?

1.   New builds of openblas fix the issue.
2.  Copying openblas build that ships with Ubuntu 16.04 fixes the issue.

## TODOs:
*  Test to see if this effects all skylake and new CPUs.
*  Contact openblas and attempt to get a fix.
*  Create a minimum reproducible example that calls openblas directly (bypass mxnet code) and pass the MRT to openblas.
*  Modify our internal docker images to use Ubuntu 16.04 as a default base.

issue_title: How to view results from recommender examples?
issue_body: I am new to MXNet and recommender engines and wanted to test out the matrix factorization demos provided with MXNet. I can run the python notebooks such as demo1-MF.ipynb just fine, but I am confused as to how to view the final output of recommendations. After training the model it plots a graph of RMSE over time, but how do I view the actual recommendations that the model should predict?

issue_title: RNN when CUDNN is disabled
issue_body:
## Description
Current RNN ops are supported only when CUDNN is enabled. We should also add RNN support with CUDNN disabled.  The new stage added by #11470 will ignore these failures for CUDNN disabled for now.

issue_title: training faster-rcnn using resnet but the test detection seems not right
issue_body:
When I  tried to apply resnet(50, 101) to faster-rcnn，it works during the training process, but seemed to fail in the testing process.

the symbol was like this:

**symbol_resnet_full.py**
[symbol_resnet_full.txt](https://github.com/dmlc/mxnet/files/594099/symbol_resnet_full.txt)


**symbol_resnet.py**
[symbol_resnet.txt](https://github.com/dmlc/mxnet/files/594100/symbol_resnet.txt)


and I trained it using e2e, changed code `sym = get_resnet(num_classes=num_classes)` in line 35.


the accuracy during training process at epoch 8 of rcnn was about 94%，and rpn about 98%，but the test result was really low with mean ap 0.22，even at the training set. I used resnet-50 pretrained model downloaded from dmlc as conv initial.

issue_title: Gluon / Autograd is_train documentation
issue_body:
As far as I understand `AutogradRuntime::Get()->IsTraining()` is used throughout the [NDArray API](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/dad83816511ec04157d2743ee1f220e74a6b0c27/src/c_api/c_api_ndarray.cc) to determine how to set the `OpContext` argument to the functions.

`with autograd.record()`sets the training attribute of AutogradRuntime to True and resets it when leaving the block.

Is this correct? This should be documented. The current Gluon documentation does not state anything about how `OpContext.is_train` is determined, even though this is very relevant to the BatchNorm and Dropout operators. If you confirm my understanding I can add a short note to the Gluon Tutorial. If a documentation page for autograd is added it would make sense to also mention it there.

issue_title: Terminating multiprocessing.Process's with pyzmq in the mix
issue_body:
Hello,

I'm currently using pyzmq to perform some basic distributed computing. To that end, I'm running code that looks something little like this:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

import sys
import time
import enum
import collections
import socket
import multiprocessing
import zmq

HOSTNAME = socket.gethostname()
WORK = range(10)
TOTAL = len(WORK)


def producer():
    print("Running as producer...")
    context = zmq.Context()
    socket = context.socket(zmq.REP)
    socket.bind("tcp://*:{}".format(5555))
    for idx, i in enumerate(WORK):
        # Wait for a consumer to ask for work.
        socket.recv_pyobj()
        socket.send_pyobj(i)
        sys.stdout.write("\r{} of {} jobs distributed".format(idx + 1, TOTAL))
        sys.stdout.flush()

def consumer():
    def inner():
        print("Running as consumer for `{}`...".format(HOSTNAME))
        context = zmq.Context()
        # Create a socket to receive work items.
        socket_task = context.socket(zmq.REQ)
        socket_task.connect(
            "tcp://{host}:{port}".format(host=HOSTNAME, port=5555))
        # Create a socket to push results downstream.
        socket_result = context.socket(zmq.PUSH)
        socket_result.connect(
            "tcp://{host}:{port}".format(host=HOSTNAME, port=5556))

        # Subscribe to the command socket.
        socket_command = context.socket(zmq.SUB)
        socket_command.connect(
            "tcp://{host}:{port}".format(host=HOSTNAME, port=5557))
        socket_command.setsockopt_string(zmq.SUBSCRIBE, "")

        # Set up a poller to receive tasks/commands.
        poller = zmq.Poller()
        poller.register(socket_task, zmq.POLLIN)
        poller.register(socket_command, zmq.POLLIN)

        while True:
            # Request work.
            socket_task.send_pyobj(None)
            # Poll for requests.
            sockets = dict(poller.poll())
            # Handle a task.
            if sockets.get(socket_task) == zmq.POLLIN:
                # Retrieve work and run the computation.
                params = socket_task.recv_pyobj()
                time.sleep(1)
                result = params
                # Send the result downstream.
                socket_result.send_pyobj(result)
            # Handle a command.
            if sockets.get(socket_command) == zmq.POLLIN:
                cmd = socket_command.recv_pyobj()
                if cmd == "stop":
                    print("Stop command received")
                    # FIXME: Child processes don't seem to terminate here.
                    break

    def Process():
        proc = multiprocessing.Process(target=inner)
        proc.daemon = True
        proc.start()
        return proc
    pool = [Process() for i in range(8)]
    # Zombies are bad for your health.
    for proc in pool:
        proc.join()

def collector():
    print("Running as collector...")

    context = zmq.Context()
    socket_result = context.socket(zmq.PULL)
    socket_result.bind("tcp://*:{}".format(5556))

    socket_command = context.socket(zmq.PUB)
    socket_command.bind("tcp://*:{}".format(5557))

    n_results = 0
    while True:
        result = socket_result.recv_pyobj()
        n_results += 1
        sys.stdout.write(
            "\r{} of {} results collected".format(n_results, TOTAL))
        sys.stdout.flush()
        if n_results == TOTAL:
            break

    # Broadcast a stop instruction to shut down worker processes.
    socket_command.send_pyobj("stop")

def main():
    role = sys.argv[1]
    if role == "producer":
        producer()
    elif role == "collector":
        collector()
    elif role == "consumer":
        consumer()
    else:
        raise SystemExit("Invalid role")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

If I run this on my local machine using
```
$ python sim.py producer
$ python sim.py collector
$ python sim.py consumer
```
(in three different terminals of course), everything works fine for the most part. Each process is receiving its work items, and each process successfully transmits its results to the collector. The problem occurs when shutting down the worker processes, which never get joined for some reason. Interrupting the consumer process shows that it is hanging at `pid, sts = os.waitpid(self.pid, flag)` in `multiprocessing/popen_fork.py`. The issue seems to be that the producer and collector terminate before the consumer(s) get a chance to. If I add a simple timeout at the end of the producer and consumer functions, every process terminates properly.

Could somebody explain to me why that is, and possibly suggest a more reliable solution than adding a random timeout?

issue_title: Train DeepSpeech on Fisher+Switchboard target WER 5-10%
Bug in sox that effects OSX native_client
issue_body:
Use of the native client on OS X exposes a sox bug that we need to work around. 

TL;DR: `fmemopen` doesn't exist on OS X. So we must require as a prerequisite a replacement, something like [memorymapping](https://github.com/NimbusKit/memorymapping), a `fmemopen` port, on OS X.

The long version...

When running the client on OS X one finds
```bash
(tensorflow) kdaviss-MBP:native_client kdavis$ ARGS="/Users/kdavis/Code/temp/DeepSpeech/export_dir/output_graph.pb /Users/kdavis/Code/temp/DeepSpeech/data/ldc93s1/LDC93S1.wav" make run
DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH=../../tensorflow/bazel-bin/tensorflow:../../tensorflow/bazel-bin/native_client: ./deepspeech /Users/kdavis/Code/temp/DeepSpeech/export_dir/output_graph.pb /Users/kdavis/Code/temp/DeepSpeech/data/ldc93s1/LDC93S1.wav
2017-03-24 12:41:03.668250: W tensorflow/core/platform/cpu_feature_guard.cc:45] The TensorFlow library wasn't compiled to use SSE4.1 instructions, but these are available on your machine and could speed up CPU computations.
2017-03-24 12:41:03.668838: W tensorflow/core/platform/cpu_feature_guard.cc:45] The TensorFlow library wasn't compiled to use SSE4.2 instructions, but these are available on your machine and could speed up CPU computations.
2017-03-24 12:41:03.668843: W tensorflow/core/platform/cpu_feature_guard.cc:45] The TensorFlow library wasn't compiled to use AVX instructions, but these are available on your machine and could speed up CPU computations.
2017-03-24 12:41:03.668847: W tensorflow/core/platform/cpu_feature_guard.cc:45] The TensorFlow library wasn't compiled to use AVX2 instructions, but these are available on your machine and could speed up CPU computations.
2017-03-24 12:41:03.668851: W tensorflow/core/platform/cpu_feature_guard.cc:45] The TensorFlow library wasn't compiled to use FMA instructions, but these are available on your machine and could speed up CPU computations.
formats: can't open output file `': No such file or directory
Assertion failed: (output), function main, file client.cc, line 102.
make: *** [run] Abort trap: 6
```
This occurs as the client's call to `sox_open_memstream_write()` fails.

In particular the native client calls `sox_open_memstream_write()` in [client.cc#L98](https://github.com/mozilla/DeepSpeech/blob/master/native_client/client.cc#L98) as follows:
```C
...
  char *buffer;
  size_t buffer_size;
  sox_format_t* output = sox_open_memstream_write(&buffer, &buffer_size,
                                                  &target_signal,
                                                  &target_encoding,
                                                  "raw", NULL);
  assert(output);
...
```
In turn `sox_open_memstream_write()` is implemented as follows [formats.c#L961](https://github.com/chirlu/sox/blob/4927023d0978615c74e8a511ce981cf4c29031f1/src/formats.c#L961)
```C
sox_format_t * sox_open_memstream_write(
    char                     * * buffer_ptr,
    size_t                   * buffer_size_ptr,
    sox_signalinfo_t   const * signal,
    sox_encodinginfo_t const * encoding,
    char               const * filetype,
    sox_oob_t          const * oob)
{
  return open_write("", NULL, (size_t)0, buffer_ptr, buffer_size_ptr, signal, encoding, filetype, oob, NULL);
}
```
Then `open_write()` is implemented as follows [formats.c#L819](https://github.com/chirlu/sox/blob/4927023d0978615c74e8a511ce981cf4c29031f1/src/formats.c#L819)
```C
static sox_format_t * open_write(
    char               const * path,
    void                     * buffer UNUSED,
    size_t                     buffer_size UNUSED,
    char                     * * buffer_ptr UNUSED,
    size_t                   * buffer_size_ptr UNUSED,
    sox_signalinfo_t   const * signal,
    sox_encodinginfo_t const * encoding,
    char               const * filetype,
    sox_oob_t          const * oob,
    sox_bool           (*overwrite_permitted)(const char *filename))
{
 ...
      ft->fp =
#ifdef HAVE_FMEMOPEN
        buffer? fmemopen(buffer, buffer_size, "w+b") :
        buffer_ptr? open_memstream(buffer_ptr, buffer_size_ptr) :
#endif
        fopen(path, "w+b");
      if (ft->fp == NULL) {
        lsx_fail("can't open output file `%s': %s", path, strerror(errno));
        goto error;
      }
    }
```
But `fmemopen()`does not exist on OS X. So this fails as `ft->fp` is `NULL` due to the fact that `path` is the empty string `""`.

My guess is that we must require as a prerequisite something like [memorymapping](https://github.com/NimbusKit/memorymapping), a `fmemopen` port, on OS X.

issue_title: Calculation is not stopped when a process is killed/terminated
issue_body:
When one of the subprocesses is killed (by the user, by the OOM killer) the computation isn't stopped and other processes continues to crunch data until the tasks have been completed.

This is quite dangerous because it leaves several processes alive using resources (or even idle but with memory allocated) and in the case of OOM killer intervention the situation get works because of the high memory pressure (i.e OOM kills one process base on scoring but this does not stop other processes to leak, depending on the memory usage rate, the OOM killer may be too slow).

One solution could be (on unix) using:
`os.killpg(os.getpgrp(), signal.SIGKILL)` in `raiseMasterKilled`

Please note the `SIGKILL`: because how `MultiProcess` works (no cancel, no revoke) a KILL is required to stop process without waiting for a task to end.

issue_title: Refactor byte conversion process to make nested serialization even more IO efficient
issue_body:
After attending the talk on bytearrays at PyCon, I had an idea:

 - Refactor the bytearray seralization steps out into a separate set of object methods (e.g. `__bytearray__`)
 - Call that from `__bytes__`
 - Now `__bytes__` should (ideally) only be called on the outermost level, which should reduce IO, as appending a bytearray to another bytearray is a much more efficient operation than converting to bytes, then back to a bigger bytearray

I don't necessarily expect this to be an insane improvement, but it should be measurable, so I plan to actually profile and benchmark the difference here as well.

Also, I think that doing this will help make the implementation of #139 a bit easier (and hopefully also more efficient than it might have been otherwise - small inefficiencies with IO in Python seem to compound very quickly as the amount of data being moved in those operations increases)

issue_title: [Question] Detecting that Yubikey is waiting for a touch via an external script
issue_body:
I have YubiKey 4 Nano and the blinking light is not always visible, so I often find myself missing a time window to touch Yubikey when there is a prompt "Please touch the device". This happens for example when I begin some long-running task that will finish with a sudo command (e.g. build and install an app).

I'm writing a script that could detect when YubiKey is waiting for a touch. I have some partial success, and it looks like this:

![yubikey_indicator](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1177900/31579601-c212a3ea-b139-11e7-818d-eed56cf7e09e.gif)

Detection for `gpg` and `sudo` operations is very different, both methods are very hacky and have their downsides, so I want to describe how I made it and ask you for suggestions, maybe you can come up with some improvement ideas.

#### GPG:
I noticed that command `gpg --card-status` normally returns instantly, but if YubiKey is waiting for a touch for another `gpg` operation (like `gpg --sign`), then `gpg --card-status` will hang until I touch the device. So I implement detection in a following way:
1. Every second or even less, try to call `gpg --card-status`.
2. If the command returns instantly, YubiKey is **not** waiting for a touch - repeat step 1
3. Otherwise, mark that YubiKey is waiting for a touch and wait until the command returns.
4. When it returns, mark that YubiKey is no longer waiting for a touch.

The problems with this approach:
- Lots of wasted CPU cycles (I want the detection to happen as fast as possible)
- The fact that YubiKey is always active, it has to respond to `gpg --card-status` a lot of times and thus the light indicator is always constantly on.

Any suggestions on how to better do this?

#### sudo (pam-u2f):
I opened [a separate question](https://github.com/Yubico/pam-u2f/issues/77) about this topic on `pam-u2f` repo, but I'll describe it here as well, as here it is a different audience and most of links on ArchWiki point to this repo anyway.

Since to configure `pam-u2f` it is needed to create `~/.config/Yubico/u2f_keys` file, it is obvious to assume that this file will be read every time Yubikey will be used. Good news, this does happen, so setting up a simple `inotify-watch` for `ACCESS` event for this file will instantly let me know when `sudo` access is being requested. No wasted CPU cycles in this approach, I like it.

However, the problem that I was unable to solve so far is how to detect that YubiKey is stopped waiting for a touch (user touched the device, or timeout happened). With `gpg` it was easy, but here I cannot find a way to detect this.

Any suggestions here?

-----

Of course it would be awesome if one of the yubikey CLI tools provided such information out of the box, it is a really useful feature in my mind and I've seen [other people requesting this](https://forum.yubico.com/viewtopic.php?f=35&t=2397). But I understand that it might be simply impossible to implement in a proper way.

issue_title: Config file for CLI
issue_body:
A config file is something quality tools have. It would allow developers to have per-directory access tokens among other things. .ini style is (see Python's distutils or https://pip.pypa.io/en/latest/user_guide.html#config-file) one way to go.

Replaces https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-sdk-py/issues/25.

issue_title: NetworkTables writes hang if flush is used more often than the update rate
issue_body:
It's a bug in ntcore: https://github.com/wpilibsuite/ntcore/issues/275

Due to the bug being pretty difficult to trigger and being almost the end of robot season, we should not push a fix for this until after Saturday.

issue_title: Dependencies of dev dependencies not properly resolving versions
issue_body:
When a default package is locked (django==1.8.18), and a dev package with a dependency (django-debug-toolbar==1.9.1 -> django>=1.8) exists, running `pipenv install --dev` doesn't properly respect the locked version (installing django 1.11 in this case).

This was brought up as part of #1137, but may be a different root cause. I've tried the following Pipfile with both 10.1.0 and current master of pipenv installed. In cases where the dev package is moved to `packages` it seems to always resolve properly.

Pipfile
```
[[source]]
url = "https://pypi.python.org/simple"
verify_ssl = true
name = "pypi"

[requires]
python_version = "2.7"

[dev-packages]
django-debug-toolbar = "==1.9.1"

[packages]
django = "==1.8.18"

issue_body:
I have tried to define two sources in Pipfile, something like this:

```toml
[[source]]
url = "https://pypi.python.org/simple"
verify_ssl = true
name = "pypi"

[[source]]
url = "https://myprivatepypi.com/simple"
verify_ssl = true
name = "internal"

[requires]
python_version = "2.7"

[dev-packages]
"coverage" = {version="*", index="pypi"}

[packages]
"my-private-package" = {version="*", index="internal"}
```

However, when I try `pipenv install` I run into an error, which includes a 404, saying that pipenv is looking for 'my-private-package' in https://pypi.python.org/simple.

https://github.com/kennethreitz/pipenv/blob/1faec7f251780e318264dee0699e6936e0c2000b/pipenv/project.py#L412-L413

Seems like the code might just be grabbing the first source in the Pipfile?

issue_title: Support `py` as an acceptable Python intpreter
issue_body: On Windows, Python is not put on `PATH` by default. Instead the `py` command is made available which operates somewhat like `pipenv --three` where a single command handles executing different Python versions. It would be great if pipenv picked up on `py` -- or Python versions installed by reading the Windows registry -- instead of only looking for `python` on `PATH`.

issue_title: Risk of outdated Pipfile.lock after editing Pipfile.
issue_body:
In addition #8, this is perhaps the most serious usability problem with the current approach: For a developer it is very easy to forget about `Pipfile.lock` after editing `Pipfile`, especially because he is not supposed to care about the lock file.

This is not an issue with pure approaches (`setup.py` or `requirements.txt`) because there is only one version of truth. Pipfile introduces an intermediate step that translates one truth into another, and different tools look at different files. This just asks for trouble. I can already see the broken builds and "fix: Forgot to build Pipfile" commits waiting to happen.

A very common workflow is to install a dependency manually, check if it works, and then add it to `requirements.txt` or just call `pip freeze` again. With Pidfiles, there is no `pip freeze` (see #8) and you have to invoke a command-line utility after editing the `Pidfile` to make things happen. Unfortunately, you just manually installed the dependency, so everything works anyway (on your computer) and there is no indication that your `Pidfile.lock` is out of sync of you forget this step.

Build or install tools (`pip`) cannot check if `Pipfile` and `Pipfile.lock` are out of sync without executing the `Pipfile` (which is bad, see #7) and cannot warn the user.

issue_title: Pickling DateTime Objects with Timezones
issue_body:
There is an error when trying to unpickle the datetime object returned from the parse function if a timezone was supplied. 

Example:

```python
import pickle

import dateparser

#
# Working with no timezones
#

# datetime.datetime(2017, 12, 15, 9, 45, 25, 600685)
dateparser.parse('now')

# b'\x80\x03cdatetime\ndatetime\nq\x00C\n\x07\xe1\x0c\x0f\t-1\r\x007q\x01\x85q\x02Rq\x03.'
pickle.dumps(dateparser.parse('now'))

# datetime.datetime(2017, 12, 15, 9, 46, 8, 611132)
pickle.loads(pickle.dumps(dateparser.parse('now')))

#
# Adding timezones
#

# datetime.datetime(2017, 12, 15, 14, 46, 30, 901640, tzinfo=<StaticTzInfo 'UTC'>)
dateparser.parse('now UTC')

# b'\x80\x03cdatetime\ndatetime\nq\x00C\n\x07\xe1\x0c\x0f\x0e.(\x0cL\x87q\x01cdateparser.timezone_parser\nStaticTzInfo\nq\x02)Rq\x03}q\x04(X\x15\x00\x00\x00_StaticTzInfo__offsetq\x05cdatetime\ntimedelta\nq\x06K\x00K\x00K\x00\x87q\x07Rq\x08X\x13\x00\x00\x00_StaticTzInfo__nameq\tX\x03\x00\x00\x00UTCq\nub\x86q\x0bRq\x0c.'
pickle.dumps(dateparser.parse('now UTC'))

pickle.loads(pickle.dumps(dateparser.parse('now UTC')))

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
# <ipython-input-7-9594dd87eef3> in <module>()
# ----> 1 pickle.loads(pickle.dumps(dateparser.parse('now UTC')))
#
# TypeError: __init__() missing 2 required positional arguments: 'name' and 'offset'
```

dateparser.timezone_parser.StaticTzInfo needs to implement __getinitargs__:

```python
    def __getinitargs__(self):
        return self.__name, self.__offset
```

issue_title: session_security breaks "End Session" on the current session when using user_sessions
issue_body:
The middleware

user_sessions.middleware.SessionMiddleware

Provides a listing of a user's sessions across various clients.  The user can then choose to "End Session" on any of their existing sessions, including their current session they are logged in as.

When using the user_sessions middleware alone, clicking "End Session" will behave the same way as "Logout".  Unfortunately, when combined with the session_security middleware, clicking "End Session" on the current session has no effect.

With some pdb tracing, I've figured out the following rough series of events:

1) request:session_security updates the last_activity in the session
2) request:user_sessions deletes the session object
3) response:user_sessions detects the session was modified in step 1) and re-saves the session to the backing DB

The user is redirected to the same Session List page, with their current session still active.

issue_body:
It might be possible to automatically quote user input when the user is tab-autocompleting a filename. Currently it's possible to autocomplete to a string containing whitespace, which triggers shell word splitting (I think):

```text
Input:   Genome (genome_fastagz)
Class:   file
Suggestions:
  project-BQpp3Y804Y0xbyG4GJPQ01xv://file-* (DNAnexus Reference Genomes: AWS US-east)
  project-Bxv84pQ27z999kj0jbQz0FK9://file-* (DNAnexus Reference Genomes: AWS China-north)

  project-F0yyz6j9Jz8YpxQV8B8Kk7Zy://file-* (DNAnexus Reference Genomes: Azure West US)

Enter file ID or path (<TAB> twice for compatible files in current directory, '?' for
more options)
genome_fastagz: 
DNAnexus GUI Workshop.pdf            References/                          SRR504516_1.fastq_200reads.fastq.gz  SRR504516_2.fastq_200reads.fastq.gz  test/

References/H. Sapiens - GRCh37 - hs37d5 (1000 Genomes Phase II)/hs37d5.2bit                 References/H. Sapiens - GRCh37 - hs37d5 (1000 Genomes Phase II)/hs37d5.fa.gz
References/H. Sapiens - GRCh37 - hs37d5 (1000 Genomes Phase II)/hs37d5.bt2-index.tar.gz     References/H. Sapiens - GRCh37 - hs37d5 (1000 Genomes Phase II)/hs37d5.fa.sa

References/H. Sapiens - GRCh37 - hs37d5 (1000 Genomes Phase II)/hs37d5.cw2-index.tar.gz     References/H. Sapiens - GRCh37 - hs37d5 (1000 Genomes Phase II)/hs37d5.star-index.tar.gz

issue_title: SystemError: return without exception set when attempting to load an oid
issue_body:
We are getting a SystemError where a POSKeyError should be raised, probably due to the exception not being set in the C code. Here is an example traceback (data is an OOBTree):

>> data.get(u'photo', None)
2018-05-25 18:19:20,336 WARNI [relstorage][MainThread] POSKeyError on oid 57955827: no tid found; history-free adapter
2018-05-25 18:19:20,337 ERROR [ZODB.Connection][MainThread] Couldn't load state for BTrees.OOBTree.OOBTree 0x037455f3
Traceback (most recent call last):
 File "/srv/osfkarl/.buildout/eggs/cp27mu/ZODB-5.2.0-py2.7.egg/ZODB/Connection.py", line 796, in setstate
   p, serial = self._storage.load(oid)
 File "/srv/osfkarl/.buildout/eggs/cp27mu/perfmetrics-2.0-py2.7.egg/perfmetrics/__init__.py", line 127, in call_with_metric
   return f(*args, **kw)
 File "/srv/osfkarl/.buildout/eggs/cp27mu/RelStorage-2.1a2-py2.7-linux-i686.egg/relstorage/storage.py", line 587, in load
   raise POSKeyError(oid)
POSKeyError: 0x037455f3

issue_title: sudo buildozer Fails
issue_body:
Yes I know you are not susposed to run Buildozer as root. But it still should not crash with a traceback.
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/bin/buildozer", line 9, in <module>
    load_entry_point('buildozer==0.26', 'console_scripts', 'buildozer')()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/buildozer/scripts/client.py", line 13, in main
    Buildozer().run_command(sys.argv[1:])
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/buildozer/__init__.py", line 946, in run_command
    self.check_root()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/buildozer/__init__.py", line 985, in check_root
    cont = input('Are you sure you want to continue [y/n]? ')
UnboundLocalError: local variable 'input' referenced before assignment
```
The lines that actually cause this error are 973-976:
```python
        try:  # ensure same result in python2 and python3
            input = raw_input
        except NameError:
            pass
```
I recommend lines 973-976 become:
```python
        try:  # ensure same result in python2 and python3
            input_func = raw_input
        except NameError:
            input_func = input
```
And line 985 becomes:
```python
cont = input_func('Are you sure you want to continue [y/n]? ')
```

issue_title: Allow setting custom serializers
issue_body:
We've got some output from DRF in dict form that has some datetime/date objects in it. DRF has a JSONRenderer class with some custom handlers for both of these, which is super handy. Keen's client, however, doesn't (that I can see).

This means we end up JSON encoding the DRF data structures, then json.loads() to get them all back in string form, then handing that off to Keen to serialize yet again. Obviously not ideal.

I'd suggest allowing the passing of an encoder class in the Client's constructor, here:

https://github.com/keenlabs/KeenClient-Python/blob/master/keen/client.py#L44

Another possibility would be to allow passing in a custom event class instead, which I could then override and set my own serializer on.

I may be able to submit a pull request for this, so let me know how you'd like to proceed.

issue_title: Overlapping drawdowns
issue_body: The `get_top_drawdowns` function in `timeseries.py` sometimes returns overlapping drawdown periods, which should not happen. This code reproduces the issue: ``` import pyfolio as pf stock_rets = pf.utils.get_symbol_rets('FB') pf.timeseries.gen_drawdown_table(stock_rets) ``` which produces the following output: Drawdown with index 6 starts on 2014-10-24, finishes on 2015-01-15 Drawdown with index 5 starts on 2014-10-28, which cannot be a peak as then it would have meant the end of the previous drawdown. Problem occurs on `timeseries.py` lines 770-772: ``` underwater = pd.concat( [underwater.loc[:peak].iloc[:-1], underwater.loc[recovery:].iloc[1:]]) ``` the actual peak is getting removed from the series, allowing a subsequent drawdown to engulf the one that just got cropped. The cropping should start on the index immediately after `peak`. Assuming that indexes are always day-based replacing line 771 by ``` [underwater.loc[:peak + pd.to_timedelta("1d")].iloc[:-1] ``` seems to fix the bug.

issue_title: memory leak: NamespaceManager
issue_body:
in its current form `NamespaceManager` has a `__cache = {}` attrib:
https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/blob/master/rdflib/namespace.py#L286

while there is a `reset()` i am living proof that this can cause days of debugging to find a memory leak... turns out `NamespaceManager` held refs to all URIs it ever saw... great.

If i find time and no one objects, i'd add `cachetools` as dependency and use a `LRUCache` and make its size configurable....

issue_title: Error validating a body parameter
issue_body:
### Description

Here is the spec.

```yaml
swagger: '2.0'
info:
  title: Connexion issue
  version: '0.0.1'
host: localhost:8080

paths:
  /:
    put:
      operationId: app.put_document
      consumes:
        - text/plain
      parameters:
        - name: body
          in: body
          required: true
          schema:
            type: string
      responses:
        200:
          description: OK
```

And here is the app.

```python
import connexion
from connexion import NoContent

def put_document(body):
    return NoContent

if __name__ == '__main__':
    app = connexion.App(__name__, port=8080)
    app.add_api('swagger.yaml')
    app.run()
```

### Expected behaviour

```
$ curl -X PUT --header 'Content-Type: text/plain' -d 'test' 'http://localhost:8080/'
```
returns `200 OK`

### Actual behaviour

Instead it returns `400 BAD REQUEST` with the following body.

```json
{
  "type": "about:blank",
  "title": "Bad Request",
  "status": 400,
  "detail": "None is not of type 'string'"
}
```

### Steps to reproduce

```sh
$ python app.py
$ curl -X PUT --header 'Content-Type: text/plain' -d 'test' 'http://localhost:8080/'
```

### Additional info:

Python 3.5.2
Version: 1.0.112

issue_title: Invalid datetime conversion (timezone)
issue_body:
Hey.
1) I set '2017-01-01 10:00:00' to field, save it to ClickHouse
2) I receive it back, and get datetime(2017, 01, 01 05:00:00).
P. s. my timezone is UTC+05:00 as you understood.
The reason is in DateTimeField:
```
    def to_db_string(self, value, quote=True):
        return escape(int(time.mktime(value.timetuple())), quote)
```
Method [time.mktime](https://docs.python.org/2/library/time.html#time.mktime) converts local time, not UTC. 
As I read from clickhouse_orm docs:
> DateTimeField. Always in UTC

Using [calendar.timegm](https://docs.python.org/2/library/calendar.html#calendar.timegm) will fix the problem, e. g.:
`calendar.timegm(value.timetuple())`

issue_title: Vérifier le calcul de la PPA pour un micro-entrepreneur (données de test)
issue_body:
Suite à un message depuis Mes Aides, pouvez-vous vérifier et éventuellement corriger le calcul d'éligibilité de la PPA pour un micro-entrepreneur ? Le point principal serait que le calcul se fait sur la moyenne des trois derniers mois et non mois par mois. Ci-joint le mail anonymisé avec les données de calcul de la Caf :

> en réponse à votre mail et après étude de votre dossier, je vous informe que vous n'ouvrez pas droit à la Prime d'Activité pour le trimestre d'Avril à Juin 2017 pour ressources supérieures au plafond.
> 
> Le calcul est fait en prenant compte des ressources perçues le trimestre précédent, soit en Janvier Février et Mars 2017.
> 
> Les ressources retenues sont:
> - les revenus non salariés de Monsieur : 1994.33 euros par mois (**ce montant correspond à la moyenne mensuelle des revenus de Monsieur** : 628+2339+3016=5983)

issue_title: Profiling data missing
issue_body:
Profiling data is lost when the context is overwritten in `Manticore._finish_run()`.

Profiling data is saved to `locked_context('profiling_stats')` **after** execution has finished. This results in locked_context returning a `Manticore._context` rather than the executor context. 
https://github.com/trailofbits/manticore/blob/61270a2bda980f79280a44407aa57b9da16f8e7d/manticore/manticore.py#L409

Which is then subsequently overwritten by the executor context.
https://github.com/trailofbits/manticore/blob/61270a2bda980f79280a44407aa57b9da16f8e7d/manticore/manticore.py#L616

But before the profiling data is saved, resulting in profiling data being empty.
https://github.com/trailofbits/manticore/blob/61270a2bda980f79280a44407aa57b9da16f8e7d/manticore/manticore.py#L619

Profiling should probably moved inside of the executor but may require some refactoring. An alternative quick fix may just be to `update()` the manticore context in `_finish_run()` rather than overwrite it. I'm not familiar enough with the rest of the inner usages to know if that will break anything else though.

issue_title: wcstools grid_from_bounding_box step should be smarter
issue_body:
The default step in `grid_from_bounding_box` is `step=1`.  But if the bounding box has more than 1 dimension, this produces an error.

The function should first check and make sure the dimensions are the same.  If the bounding box has ndim > 1 and step ndim = 1, then assume the step is the same in all dimensions and proceed accordingly.  In other words, the default should be `step=1` for all dimensions of the input bounding box.

The following code should work:
```
from gwcs import wcstools

bounding_box = ((0, 2047), (0, 51))
grid = wcstools.grid_from_bounding_box(bounding_box)
```

issue_title: Connection reset by peer in logging client libs
issue_body:
I use gcloud logging and datastore client libs on my GCE VMs. Sometimes (rather sporadically) my jobs fail due to connection reset by peer. The only common pattern is that it happens in long running jobs.

The stacktrace goes something like this:

>   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/gcloud/logging/logger.py", line 189, in log_text
>     client.logging_api.write_entries([entry_resource])
>   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/gcloud/logging/connection.py", line 150, in write_entries
>     data=data)
>   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/gcloud/connection.py", line 343, in api_request
>     target_object=_target_object)
>   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/gcloud/connection.py", line 241, in _make_request
>     return self._do_request(method, url, headers, data, target_object)
>   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/gcloud/connection.py", line 270, in _do_request
>     body=data)
>   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/oauth2client/transport.py", line 169, in new_request
>     redirections, connection_type)
>   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/httplib2/__init__.py", line 1609, in request
>     (response, content) = self._request(conn, authority, uri, request_uri, method, body, headers, redirections, cachekey)
>   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/httplib2/__init__.py", line 1351, in _request
>     (response, content) = self._conn_request(conn, request_uri, method, body, headers)
>   File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/httplib2/__init__.py", line 1307, in _conn_request
>     response = conn.getresponse()
>   File "/usr/lib/python2.7/httplib.py", line 1073, in getresponse
>     response.begin()
>   File "/usr/lib/python2.7/httplib.py", line 415, in begin
>     version, status, reason = self._read_status()
>   File "/usr/lib/python2.7/httplib.py", line 371, in _read_status
>     line = self.fp.readline(_MAXLINE + 1)
>   File "/usr/lib/python2.7/socket.py", line 476, in readline
>     data = self._sock.recv(self._rbufsize)
>   File "/usr/lib/python2.7/ssl.py", line 714, in recv
>     return self.read(buflen)
>   File "/usr/lib/python2.7/ssl.py", line 608, in read
>     v = self._sslobj.read(len or 1024)
> error: [Errno 104] Connection reset by peer 

I'm not totally sure, but this could be because of the connection pool being maintained by the underlying httplib2 library. httplib2 is holding connection pools, but doesn't seem to be refreshing them. I suspect some of my datastore requests are also failing due to a similar issue.

issue_title: Support for full bundling of native dependencies / static linking
issue_body:
Pex is cool when it comes to pure Python modules, and cases where you have control over the target environment. This is for example not the case for code to be deployed at AWS Lambda for example, or if you want to share your shiny PyQt-based app with some friends.

It would be very nice if Pex had support for gathering of dependencies, and setting up the appropriate library paths etc for the application to be able to execute fully from within the Pex file based on the OS/architecture it's executed under.

Without the support there will just be more and more projects like this:
https://github.com/moesy/AWS-Lambda-ML-Microservice-Skeleton
https://github.com/Miserlou/lambda-packages

It might be that this is a deeper problem with Python, and how Python modules are built, but to me as a user, it's what I expect to see when reading the description:

> "...and makes the deployment of Python applications as simple as cp. pex files may even include multiple platform-specific Python distributions, meaning that a single pex file can be portable across Linux and OS X..."

issue_title: Give zinc wrapper ability to create jar files
issue_body:
The zinc wrapper we have at https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/tree/3dc6cce95c5e38c75b77b81ebd4ca01a74dff9ea/src/scala/org/pantsbuild/zinc/compiler currently takes a flag `_classesDirectory` in `Settings`.

We should give it a new flag, `outputJar`. If it is specified, we should jar up the contents of `_classesDirectory` (using the standard `java.util.jar.JarOutputStream`, unless there's a good reason not to) and output it to the path pointed to by that flag.

We should use a single, deterministic timestamp for each entry which we create, so that the jarfiles are byte-for-byte reproducible.

Maybe we want to make `classesDIrectory` an optional flag, such that the valid combinations are:
1. `classesDirectory` set, `outputJar` not - the current behaviour.
2. `classesDirectory` and `outputJar` both set - classes will be in the former, jar file at the latter
3. `outputJar` set, `classesDirectory` not - classes will be created in a tempdir, jar'd, and then deleted.

And an error results if neither are set.

issue_title: Create an example that forces dealing with multiple promises.
issue_body: Right now the scheduler disallows multiple promises and blows up, but @stuhood pointed out a case where this is needed, namely for "jvm" targets that define a [`ServiceLoader` service](http://docs.oracle.com/javase/7/docs/api/java/util/ServiceLoader.html).  These targets will have 2 natural classpath products, their compiled classfiles and their `META-INF/services/...` resource file.  It makes sense that seperate planners/tasks should handle each of these: javac and scalac to generate classfiles for the jvm targets and a service-info resource file generator.  Add an example that requires handling both of these products and experiment with scheduler/planner/product type adaptations that allow for it while still disallowing multiple products in other cases where it is dangerous and/or confusing; eg having both scrooge and apache thrift generate java stubs for a given set of thrift files.

issue_title: Create a prototype engine and experiment system.
issue_body: This will flesh out the front-end of the new engine described [here](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1MQLmVGHLnA2xlVgnFjwQQeFZRonTbxM1FyBS5sYwyr8/edit#heading=h.cpc49vhr5llc) and serve as place to crystallize the API boundary between the engine planner frontend and execution backends (local, remote, ...).

issue_title: Travis not sanity checking contrib module consumption
issue_body:
As part of #4992, I adjusted travis to build all modules on the binary builder shards, but to skip actually running the tests for all contrib modules (because, in particular: the android and node contrib modules take a long time to test, and the tests run on other shards).

But it appears that we lost coverage related to whether a module is actually importable as a plugin (as opposed to being loadable when it is located directly on the PYTHONPATH), and so as of 7c92a6f8d1ec7397b14c41bcd3519fc0d14ea624, running `build-support/bin/release.sh -n` fails with something like:
```
Exception message: 'module' object has no attribute 'build_file_aliases'
Failed to install and test package pantsbuild.pants.contrib.confluence-1.4.0rc0+0c69e778!
Dry run release failed.
```

I think a middle ground might be to simplify the "test that a plugin is usable" blocks significantly, but to remove the `-c` flag, and run sanity checks on the binary builder shards.

issue_title: Prefer wheels during plugin install
issue_body:
Pants has sprouted a dependency on a package that is most easily installed via a `whl` on pypi (`openssl` for `requests[security]`).

But currently the plugin installation infrastructure does not enable usage of `WheelPackage` due to a defensive avoidance of assuming that `wheel` is installed in an environment that pants is being loaded from:
https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/e0d5108ff75a41421a11321c73c817332f0a1c86/src/python/pants/init/plugin_resolver.py#L79-L82

As far as @kwlzn or I can tell, `wheel` should always be on the PYTHONPATH, as pants itself depends on it: it's not clear that it actually needs to be "installed" on the PATH as well.

cc @jsirois for any context he might have.

issue_title: Performance comparison between training and translation
issue_body:
Hi guys.

Actually I'm running Sockeye with WMT_German_to_English dataset. I would like to compare performance of training vs inference (translation). Training mode returns "samples/sec" metric, while translation returns "sent/sec" metric. Is there any way to compare those two metrics? Or maybe modify translation's output to also provide "samples/s" value?

Best regards,
Wadim

issue_title: Enable protected branch for master?
issue_body:
Protected branches are a relatively new and handy feature:

https://github.com/blog/2051-protected-branches-and-required-status-checks

At the very least I think we should enable these for `master`:

* Can't be force pushed
* Can't be deleted

This is also handy too:

* Can't have changes merged into them until required status checks pass

issue_title: [Feature request] Support for YAML anchors in SAM/CF templates
issue_body:
Any chance we can get [YAML anchor](https://learnxinyminutes.com/docs/yaml/) support for SAM?

As it is, YAML anchors *are* supported in Amazon Swagger S3 files referenced in SAM files via, e.g., `DefinitionUri`. This is awesome as it lets us very succinctly define new API Gateway endpoints. For example:

```
    /forgot:                                                                                                      
      put:
        <<: *scaffold                                                                                             
        x-amazon-apigateway-integration:                                                                          
          <<: *integration
          uri: "arn:aws:apigateway:us-east-1:lambda:path/2015-03-31/functions/arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:XXX/invocations"                                                                   
      options: *options
```

Beyond Swagger YAML anchor support, anchors don't work in CF/SAM files currently.
